intro:
The Ten Commandments (Hebrew aseret ha-devarim, the ten words) are the foundational covenant stipulations given by God to Israel at Sinai, inscribed by God on two stone tablets and preserved in the ark of the covenant (Exodus 34:28; Deuteronomy 10:4). They appear in two closely parallel forms: Exodus 20:1–17 and Deuteronomy 5:6–21. The Decalogue addresses the whole community of Israel and covers duties toward God (the first table: exclusive allegiance, no images, honoring the divine name, Sabbath) and duties toward neighbor (the second table: honoring parents, prohibitions against murder, adultery, theft, false witness, and covetousness).

Jesus summarized the Decalogue under the two great commandments — love God with all one's heart and love the neighbor as oneself — while also deepening its demands to include the inner disposition behind the external act (Matthew 5:21–48). Paul teaches that the commandments are fulfilled through love (Romans 13:8–10). The Reformed tradition has generally held the Decalogue as a permanent moral standard applicable to all people in all ages, while other theological traditions have debated the continuing status of the Sabbath commandment in particular. The tablets were stored in the ark and kept at Sinai, in the tabernacle, and ultimately in Solomon's temple (1 Kings 8:9).
